content.text: Offline Queuing of Claude Requests in React Native

Mobile devices lose connectivity constantly — in tunnels, elevators, rural areas, or simply when a user's data plan runs out. An app that silently drops the user's Claude request in these moments feels broken. The better pattern is to accept the request anyway, persist it to a durable local queue, and flush it automatically the moment connectivity is restored. This document implements that pattern end-to-end using

attrs.alt: Offline queue state machine: ONLINE sends immediately; OFFLINE enqueues to AsyncStorage; on reconnect flushes queue

Reading and Writing the Persistent Queue
Wrap all queue I/O in pure functions. Keeping these separate from React state makes them testable and reusable in any context:

When connectivity is restored, drain the queue one item at a time. Processing serially avoids overwhelming the API and makes retry logic straightforward:

Re-queuing on every error is a mistake. A
won't fix itself when connectivity returns — drop these immediately and surface the error to the user.
A message queued hours ago may be out of context by the time it sends. Apply a
check on flush and notify the user if items were dropped.

The queue item captures conversation history at enqueue time. If new messages arrive while offline, the queued item's history is outdated. Re-build the history from current state on flush rather than preserving a stale snapshot.
NetInfo takes a moment to report the first state. Treat
as offline; do not attempt a flush until the first
Persist queued requests to AsyncStorage — not component state — so they survive app restarts and process kills.
Use NetInfo to detect connectivity changes and trigger a flush the moment the device comes back online.
Flush serially, retry only on transient network errors (5xx / timeout), and drop items after
status in the chat bubble and an offline banner — users should never wonder whether their message was accepted.
Serialize all queue reads and writes behind a mutex to prevent race conditions between concurrent enqueue and flush operations.
